0: Environmental impacts of a Japanese dairy farming system using whole-crop rice silage as evaluated by life cycle assessment
Akifumi OGINO, 1 Motohiko ISHIDA, 2 Tetsuya ISHIKAWA, 2 Atsuo IKEGUCHI, 1 Miyoko WAKI, Hiroshi YOKOYAMA, 1 Yasuo TANAKA ¹ and Hiroyuki HIROOKA3
National Institute of Livestock and Grassland Science, National Agricultural Research Center, Tsukuba and 3Graduate School of Agriculture, Kyoto University, Sakyo, Kyoto, Japan
1: The objectives of this study were to assess and compare the environmental impacts of two types of dairy farming systems, one of which makes use of whole-crop rice silage and the other of which is conventional, using life cycle assessment (LCA). The functional unit was defined as 1 kg of 4% fat-corrected milk (FCM). The processes associated with the dairy farming life cycle, such as feed production, feed transport, animal management including biological activity of the animal, and waste treatment were included within the system's boundaries. Environmental impacts of the rice silage-using and conventional dairy farming systems were 987 and 972 g CO2 equivalents for global warming, 6.87 and 7.13 g SO2 equivalents for acidification, 1.19 and 1.23 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication, and 5.53 and 5.81 MJ for energy consumption, respectively. Our results suggest that the dairy farming system using rice silage in Japan has smaller environmental impacts for acidification, eutrophication, and energy consumption, and a larger impact for global warming compared with conventional farming. Further interpretation integrating these impact categories suggested 1.1% lower environmental impact of the rice silage-using dairy farming system as a whole.
Key words: dairy farming, domestic feed production, environmental impact, life cycle assessment, rice silage.

3: Intensive animal production has been expanding in recent decades in Japan, and Japan is importing a huge amount of animal feed including forages. Domestic production of feed for domestically raised animals is an obvious way to improve the country's low food self-sufficiency rate and to prevent an excess of animal waste. Rice is the most widely produced crop in Japan, and there are extensive areas of rice paddy fields, although the human consumption of rice has been decreasing. Paddy fields have many other beneficial functions such as retention of ground water, control of air temperature, and prevention of flood- ing. Forage rice is a feed crop suitable for the humid climates of East Asia, including Japan, and utilizes paddy fields under production adjustment programs

for rice. The production of forage rice as whole-crop silage and the application of composted cattle manure in paddy fields are therefore desirable solutions to shifting food demands. Research has been conducted to determine the best methods of producing high- quality whole-crop rice silage (Islam et al. 2004a; Maruyama et al. 2005) and to analyze suitable feeding of whole-crop rice silage to cattle (Islam et al. 2004b). However, to the best of our knowledge, the environ- mental aspects of using rice silage have not yet been investigated. In the present era of increasing environmental consciousness, the quantification and

evaluation of environmental impacts of agricultural activities, including the effects of biomass circulation, are important.

Life cycle assessment (LCA), which has been standardized internationally (ISO 1997, 1998), is a type of integrated environmental impact assessment. Recently, several studies have reported that LCA can be applied to the assessment of dairy farming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; Casey & Holden 2005), and several animal production and related been evaluated using LCA (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007a). LCA has therefore been shown to be appropriate for evaluating the environmental impacts of the use of rice silage in dairy farming.

The objectives of the present study were to assess the environmental impacts of two types of dairy farming systems using LCA: One system using rice silage and a conventional system. Also, the assessed environmental impacts of the two systems were to be compared.

6: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the analysis, the functional unit, and the system boundaries. The functional unit is a reference to which all other materials (and also associated environmental loads) in the LCA are related. Examples of impact categories are global warming, acidification, and eutrophication. In the present study, the

goal of the analysis was to evaluate and compare the envi- ronmental impacts of two types of Japanese dairy farming systems: one of which makes use of whole-crop rice silage (WCRS) and the other of which is conventional (CNV). An outline of the systems analyzed in this study is presented in Figure 1. The basic dairy production cycle was defined as follows: a heifer was assumed to deliver for the first time at 26 months of age, and an average of 3.94 calves were pro- duced per cow with a calving interval of 433 days. The average calving interval was determined based on dairy herd improvement records (LIAJ 2005), and the average number of calves per cow was calculated using the calving interval and the average slaughter age estimated on the basis of the number of COWS of each age (MAFF 2005a). It was assumed that all produced calves were shipped out, with the exception of a female calf that was to be reared as a replacement heifer for the system. A culled cow was also shipped. The weight of both lactating and dry COWS was assumed to be 650 kg, and the milk yield of lactating COWS was defined as 35 kg/day of milk with an average of 3.5% fat content. The functional unit was defined as 1 kg of 4% fat-corrected milk (FCM). FCM was calculated using the following equation based on a milk fat percentage of 3.5% (MAFF 1999):
FCM = amount of milk X (15: milk fat percentage/ 100+0.4)

Diets for the COWS and heifers were designed on the basis of the nutrient requirements for dairy cattle and actual man- agement of Japanese dairy farming (MAFF 1999), SO that the diets for the WCRS and CNV systems had equal nutritional value. The lactating COWS were fed corn silage-based diets during the winter and Italian ryegrass silage-based diets during the summer, and the dry COWS were fed Sudangrass hay-based diets. Diets for the rice silage system included rice silage to the extent that neither the lactation of lactating COWS nor the growth of heifers was adversely affected, based on data obtained in previous cattle experiments (JGAFSA 2006), and timothy hay was fed as an alternative in the conventional system. The diets for the dry COWS and heifers during the period prior to parturition were supplemented according to previously determined additional nutrient requirements (MAFF 1999). A growth curve for the heifers was obtained on the basis of data for dairy heifers in the literature (MAFF 1999), and was used to calculate the nutrient requirements and amounts of nitrogen and carbon excreted into manure. In the present dairy farming systems silages were produced domestically, and other feeds such as hay and concentrate were purchased. Cattle were fed these feeds in a barn.

Finished compost was used as organic fertilizer for silage production in the systems. Any surplus was used on other farmlands as organic fertilizer and was not included in the present systems. The environmental loads associated with the production of capital goods such as cattle barns and front loaders were not taken into account.

Multiple products (milk, surplus or male calves, and culled cows) are produced in dairy farming systems, and thus economic allocation was conducted. The sale prices of milk, market calves, and a culled cow throughout the life cycle of a cow were determined as 3622 thousand, 144 thousand, and 126 thousand yen, respectively, on the basis of statistics for production costs (MAFF 2005b) and for animal products (MAFF 2005c). The proportion of environmental loads of milk was consequently calculated to be 93.1% of the total.

FCM = amount of milk X (15: milk fat percentage/ 100+0.4)

Multiple products (milk, surplus or male calves, and culled cows) are produced in dairy farming systems, and thus economic allocation was conducted. The sale prices of milk, market calves, and a culled cow throughout the life cycle of a cow were determined as 3622 thousand, 144 thousand, and 126 thousand yen, respectively, on the basis of statistics for production costs (MAFF 2005b) and for animal products (MAFF 2005c). The proportion of environmental loads of milk was consequently calculated to be 93.1% of the total.

17: The first step of LCA is the definition of the goal and scope of the analysis, the functional unit, and the system boundaries. The functional unit is a reference to which all other materials (and also associated environmental loads) in the LCA are related. Examples of impact categories are global warming, acidification, and eutrophication. In the present study, the

goal of the analysis was to evaluate and compare the envi- ronmental impacts of two types of Japanese dairy farming systems: one of which makes use of whole-crop rice silage (WCRS) and the other of which is conventional (CNV). An outline of the systems analyzed in this study is presented in Figure 1. The basic dairy production cycle was defined as follows: a heifer was assumed to deliver for the first time at 26 months of age, and an average of 3.94 calves were pro- duced per cow with a calving interval of 433 days. The average calving interval was determined based on dairy herd improvement records (LIAJ 2005), and the average number of calves per cow was calculated using the calving interval and the average slaughter age estimated on the basis of the number of COWS of each age (MAFF 2005a). It was assumed that all produced calves were shipped out, with the exception of a female calf that was to be reared as a replacement heifer for the system. A culled cow was also shipped. The weight of both lactating and dry COWS was assumed to be 650 kg, and the milk yield of lactating COWS was defined as 35 kg/day of milk with an average of 3.5% fat content. The functional unit was defined as 1 kg of 4% fat-corrected milk (FCM). FCM was calculated using the following equation based on a milk fat percentage of 3.5% (MAFF 1999):
FCM = amount of milk X (15: milk fat percentage/ 100+0.4)

Diets for the COWS and heifers were designed on the basis of the nutrient requirements for dairy cattle and actual man- agement of Japanese dairy farming (MAFF 1999), SO that the diets for the WCRS and CNV systems had equal nutritional value. 19: The second step of LCA is to draw up an inventory of all the resources used and all the emissions released into the envi- ronment connected with all processes within the system boundary. All inputs and outputs associated with dairy

farming considered in the analysis, such as feed production, feed transport, animal management, biological activities of the animals, and waste treatment are shown in Table 5. The output coefficients used in this study were adopted because they were determined to be most appropriate to the defined conditions of the processes in the systems analyzed. These included climate, animals, and styles of housing and waste treatment. For example, the regression equation suggested by the Japanese researchers were used to calculate enteric CH4 emission by cattle taking account of the ability of Japa- nese cattle (Shibata et al. 1993), and the coefficient suggested by Parkinson et al. 2004 was used to calculate NH3 emission from composting, considering the condition of composting such as scale, turning, and no forced aeration. Data from the literature were used for specific activities of dairy farming, while databases of the LCA software JEMAI-LCA (JEMAI 2000) and SimaPro (PRe Consultants 2003) were used for general activities such as the production and combustion of fossil fuels and feed transport. Levels of pollutants emitted from the production of the purchased feeds were determined using data from the United States reported by Pimentel (Pimentel 1980) as described by Ogino et al. (2004, 2007b).

Pollutant emissions from domestic silage production were determined using data concerning the inputs of materials and energy and relevant emissions reported by NIAES (NIAES 2003); missing data on amounts of agricultural mate- rials used were obtained from another source (AFFTIS 1997). For rice silage production, inputs and outputs per hectare were determined on the basis of field experiments or samples of actual cultivation as follows: yield 10 000 kg-DM; gasoline 51.3 L; diesel oil 98.1 L; electricity 100 kWh; chemical fertil- izer 60.4 kg-N and kg-K2O; calcium silicate 1200 kg; seed 35.0 kg; herbicide 10.0 kg; compost 20 000 kg. Environ- mental loads were calculated in reference to NIAES 2003 using these inputs and outputs. As for N2O emissions from the production of chemical nitrogen fertilizer, the proportion of NO3-N to the total nitrogen in chemical fertilizer was determined as 9.49% based on the statistics of fertilizer use (USDA 2006), and 1.58% of the nitrogen amount in nitric acid was assumed to be emitted as N2O during the production of nitric acid (IPCC 1997).

Environmental loads from feed transport were determined by multiplying unit emissions by the product of feed weight and transport distance. It was assumed that all purchased feeds such as concentrates and hay were imported from the United States, on the basis of Japanese trade statistics. The marine transport distance from the United States was defined as 18 180 km, based on the route from New Orleans to Nagoya and including river freight down the Mississippi River. The distance between these cities was based on the JNOA distance chart (JNOA 1990). The land transport dis- tances (trucking) in the United States and Japan were defined as 200 km and 210 km, respectively. Silage was assumed to be produced in the region where the dairy farm was located, and the land transport distance was determined to be 30 km.

Lighting of the cattle barn, feed preparation, and carrying manure out of the barn were taken into account as work associated with animal management, and environmental loads were calculated based on the quantities of fuel and electricity consumed in the work (AFFTIS 2000). For waste treatment, environmental loads relevant to fossil fuel energy were calculated based on the quantities of fuel and electricity consumed during composting (AFFTIS 2000), and those emitted in association with the degradation of manure were determined using the emission factors shown in Table 5.
Enteric CH4 emissions by cattle were calculated from dry matter intake (DMI) using the following quadratic regression equation reported by Shibata et al. 1993:
L CH4 production/day = DMI/day) - 0.849 x (kg DMI/day)2 (2)
For calves under four months of age, however, the CH4 emissions were calculated as a function of week of age using the following regression equation reported by Sekine et al. 1986:
The amounts of feces were determined based on the digest- ibility of the ingredients in the diets. Nitrogen content in

excreted feces and urine per kg of FCM for lactating COWS (expressed in g/kg) was calculated from kg FCM/day, %CP of DM feed, and kg DMI/day of cattle using the following multiple regression equation reported by Terada et al. 1997:
Excreted N = -14.48 In (FCM) + 0.806 X CP + 0.769 X DMI + 31.4 (4)
Nitrogen content in excreted feces and urine for dry COWS, heifer, and calves (expressed in g/day) was calculated as follows (MAFF 1999):

where CP is crude protein intake (g/day), RP is retained protein (g/day), and PP is pregnancy protein (g/day) calcu- lated using the feeding standard (MAFF 1999). Organic matter content in excreted feces was calculated from the composition and the digestibility of each nutrient of the diets using the standard tables of feed composition (NARO 2002). The amount and composition of diets were set to meet nutrient requirements of cattle in response to milk yield or growth stage. The amount of manure and enteric CH4 emission were calculated from the diets. Environmental loads related to manure were calculated by multiplying the nitrogen or organic matter in the manure by the coefficients. Production and environmental loads were therefore related to each other.

Based on these data energy, CO2, CH4, N20, NH3, NOx and SO2 were calculated either as resources or as emissions. Emis- sions of CO2 from cattle respiration and the composting of cattle waste were offset by carbon fixation through photo- synthesis from the atmosphere into forage crops. 26: In the present study, the two different dairy farming systems were evaluated in terms of their contributions to the follow- ing environmental impact categories: global warming, acidi- fication, eutrophication and energy consumption. The global warming potential (GWP), an index for estimating global warming contribution due to atmospheric emission of green- house gases, was calculated according to the CO2-equivalent factors given by the IPCC (2001) for CO2 = 1, CH4 = 23 and N2O = 296. These factors were set based on a time horizon of 100 years. To calculate the acidification potential of the dif- ferent trace gases, the SO2-equivalent factors for SO2 = 1, NOx 0.7 and NH3 = 1.88 derived from Heijungs et al. (Heijungs et al. 1992) were used. To calculate the eutrophi- cation potential, the PO4-equivalent factors derived from Heijungs et al. 1992 for NOx = 0.13 and NH3 = 0.33 were used.

As an option for further interpretation of the LCA results, weighting and integration of the environmental impact cat- legories were conducted using the life-cycle impact assess- ment method based on endpoint modeling (LIME). CO2 emissions from feed production and N2O emissions from waste treat- ment were also major sources. For acidification and eutrophication, NH3 emissions derived from fertilizers applied to the fields in feed production and from cattle manure in animal management and waste treatment were the prominent factors. With respect to global warming, WCRS showed smaller CO2 emissions than CNV in feed trans- port due to the shorter transport distance of feed caused by the change from imported hay to regionally produced rice silage. However, a larger amount of CH4 was emitted from paddy fields of forage rice for WCRS, while CNV feed production produced no CH4 emis- sions. Therefore, WCRS was environmentally inferior for global warming due to the much larger greenhouse effect of CH4. Many research results have been

reported on methods to achieve possible reductions in CH4 emissions from rice paddy fields by water man- agement (Yagi et al. 1996; Minamikawa & Sakai 2006), management of organic matter (Yagi et al. 1997; Liou et al. 2003), and the application of specific fertilizers or materials (Bronson & Mosier 1991; Minamikawa et al. 2005). The adoption of these techniques may reduce the impact on global warming of rice production. In addition, under the WCRS system, compost made from cattle manure rather than rice straw is applied to rice paddy fields as organic fertilizer due to the use of rice as whole-crop silage, and compost application has a smaller CH4 emission than rice straw application (Yagi et al. 1997). Forage rice production therefore appears to have a smaller contribution to global warming than grain rice production, in which rice straw residues are often applied.
In contrast to the case of global warming, WCRS showed a smaller contribution to acidification and

eutrophication, and NH3 emissions from feed produc- tion were smaller for WCRS than for CNV in these impact categories. This is mainly due to the fact that rice silage production uses smaller amounts of chemi- cal fertilizer per yield than hay production, because NH3 is emitted from chemical fertilizers applied to the field in feed production. WCRS also consumed smaller amounts of energy than CNV throughout the entire dairy farming system, primarily due to the lower energy consumption for the shorter-distance feed transport in WCRS. In the comparison between WCRS and CNV, energy consumption produced the largest advantage for WCRS (4.8% reduction) among the environmental impact categories investigated. In contrast to other impact categories for which pro- cesses such as animal management and waste treat- ment had large contributions, feed production and feed transport comprise the largest part of energy consumption in the dairy farming systems, and therefore the smaller energy consumption in feed transport under the WCRS system led to lower energy consumption throughout the whole life cycle of dairy farming. The present LIME results suggest that WCRS has a smaller overall environmental impact, although the total difference between the two dairy farming systems is small. In the LIME evaluation, global warming was the category in which the largest effect on the total impact was seen, followed by acidifica- tion. Therefore WCRS, which emitted more green- house gases, showed only slight overall superiority over CNV. This is not unexpected, because global warming is very likely the most emphasized environ- mental impact category in modern Japanese society, and its weighting in LIME reflects the awareness of Japanese citizens of this issue (Itsubo et al. 2004). Other advantages of rice silage are that existing paddy fields for grain rice production are available without any modification, and that rice silage pro- duction may be changed to grain rice production in case of a possible future lack of grain rice. However, these advantages are difficult to evaluate from an environmental point of view. In addition, a larger amount of the produced compost was self-consumed in WCRS. In the dairy farming systems analyzed in this study, WCRS used 27.6% of the produced compost in contrast to CNV, in which 10.3% of the produced compost was used, and thus use of rice silage would lead to improvement of material|Environmental impacts of the dairy farming systems With respect to global warming, enteric CH4 emissions were the largest contributor in this study, as in Japanese beef production. However the estimated proportions were about 36%, smaller than that of beef production, which has been reported to be 48-61% (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007b). It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. This is because of the quadratic relationship between CH4 emissions and DMI. The processes of waste treatment accounted for about 21% of the total contribution to global warming. These proportions were larger than those in Japanese beef fattening (10%) (Ogino et al. 2004) and in beef cow-calf production (11%) (Ogino et al. 2007b). This difference might be caused by the high moisture content of dairy cattle manure, which is likely to lead to large N2O and CH4 emissions. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. Many research results on the LCA of milk have been reported, and environmental impacts per kg-milk range from 689 to 1300 g CO2 equivalents for global warming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Casey & Holden 2005; Masuda et al. 2005), from 3.5 to 22.0 SO2 equivalents for acidification (Cederberg & Matts- son 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda al. 2005), from 2.8 to 28.8 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda et al. 2005), and from 1.2 to 3.9 MJ for energy consumption (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003). However, different assumptions, conditions, and emis- sion factors were applied in these different studies, and the functional units were also different (milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), and FCM). Precise comparison is therefore difficult, but the present results are almost consistent with the previously reported values. Our results suggest that use of rice silage in dairy farming has a lower overall environmental impact than the conventional system, although the difference is small. With respect to global warming, WCRS showed smaller CO2 emissions than CNV in feed trans- port due to the shorter transport distance of feed caused by the change from imported hay to regionally produced rice silage. However, a larger amount of CH4 was emitted from paddy fields of forage rice for WCRS, while CNV feed production produced no CH4 emis- sions. Therefore, WCRS was environmentally inferior for global warming due to the much larger greenhouse effect of CH4. Many research results have been

reported on methods to achieve possible reductions in CH4 emissions from rice paddy fields by water man- agement (Yagi et al. 1996; Minamikawa & Sakai 2006), management of organic matter (Yagi et al. 1997; Liou et al. 2003), and the application of specific fertilizers or materials (Bronson & Mosier 1991; Minamikawa et al. 2005). The adoption of these techniques may reduce the impact on global warming of rice production. In addition, under the WCRS system, compost made from cattle manure rather than rice straw is applied to rice paddy fields as organic fertilizer due to the use of rice as whole-crop silage, and compost application has a smaller CH4 emission than rice straw application (Yagi et al. 1997). Forage rice production therefore appears to have a smaller contribution to global warming than grain rice production, in which rice straw residues are often applied.
In contrast to the case of global warming, WCRS showed a smaller contribution to acidification and

eutrophication, and NH3 emissions from feed produc- tion were smaller for WCRS than for CNV in these impact categories. This is mainly due to the fact that rice silage production uses smaller amounts of chemi- cal fertilizer per yield than hay production, because NH3 is emitted from chemical fertilizers applied to the field in feed production. WCRS also consumed smaller amounts of energy than CNV throughout the entire dairy farming system, primarily due to the lower energy consumption for the shorter-distance feed transport in WCRS. In the comparison between WCRS and CNV, energy consumption produced the largest advantage for WCRS (4.8% reduction) among the environmental impact categories investigated. In contrast to other impact categories for which pro- cesses such as animal management and waste treat- ment had large contributions, feed production and feed transport comprise the largest part of energy consumption in the dairy farming systems, and therefore the smaller energy consumption in feed transport under the WCRS system led to lower energy consumption throughout the whole life cycle of dairy farming. The present LIME results suggest that WCRS has a smaller overall environmental impact, although the total difference between the two dairy farming systems is small. In the LIME evaluation, global warming was the category in which the largest effect on the total impact was seen, followed by acidifica- tion. Therefore WCRS, which emitted more green- house gases, showed only slight overall superiority over CNV. This is not unexpected, because global warming is very likely the most emphasized environ- mental impact category in modern Japanese society, and its weighting in LIME reflects the awareness of Japanese citizens of this issue (Itsubo et al. 2004). Other advantages of rice silage are that existing paddy fields for grain rice production are available without any modification, and that rice silage pro- duction may be changed to grain rice production in case of a possible future lack of grain rice. However, these advantages are difficult to evaluate from an environmental point of view. In addition, a larger amount of the produced compost was self-consumed in WCRS. In the dairy farming systems analyzed in this study, WCRS used 27.6% of the produced compost in contrast to CNV, in which 10.3% of the produced compost was used, and thus use of rice silage would lead to improvement of material|Environmental impacts of the dairy farming systems With respect to global warming, enteric CH4 emissions were the largest contributor in this study, as in Japanese beef production. However the estimated proportions were about 36%, smaller than that of beef production, which has been reported to be 48-61% (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007b). It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. This is because of the quadratic relationship between CH4 emissions and DMI. The processes of waste treatment accounted for about 21% of the total contribution to global warming. These proportions were larger than those in Japanese beef fattening (10%) (Ogino et al. 2004) and in beef cow-calf production (11%) (Ogino et al. 2007b). This difference might be caused by the high moisture content of dairy cattle manure, which is likely to lead to large N2O and CH4 emissions. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. Many research results on the LCA of milk have been reported, and environmental impacts per kg-milk range from 689 to 1300 g CO2 equivalents for global warming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Casey & Holden 2005; Masuda et al. 2005), from 3.5 to 22.0 SO2 equivalents for acidification (Cederberg & Matts- son 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda al. 2005), from 2.8 to 28.8 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda et al. 2005), and from 1.2 to 3.9 MJ for energy consumption (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003). However, different assumptions, conditions, and emis- sion factors were applied in these different studies, and the functional units were also different (milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), and FCM). Precise comparison is therefore difficult, but the present results are almost consistent with the previously reported values. Our results suggest that use of rice silage in dairy farming has a lower overall environmental impact than the conventional system, although the difference is small. The adoption of these techniques may reduce the impact on global warming of rice production. In addition, under the WCRS system, compost made from cattle manure rather than rice straw is applied to rice paddy fields as organic fertilizer due to the use of rice as whole-crop silage, and compost application has a smaller CH4 emission than rice straw application (Yagi et al. 1997). Forage rice production therefore appears to have a smaller contribution to global warming than grain rice production, in which rice straw residues are often applied.
In contrast to the case of global warming, WCRS showed a smaller contribution to acidification and

eutrophication, and NH3 emissions from feed produc- tion were smaller for WCRS than for CNV in these impact categories. This is mainly due to the fact that rice silage production uses smaller amounts of chemi- cal fertilizer per yield than hay production, because NH3 is emitted from chemical fertilizers applied to the field in feed production. WCRS also consumed smaller amounts of energy than CNV throughout the entire dairy farming system, primarily due to the lower energy consumption for the shorter-distance feed transport in WCRS. In the comparison between WCRS and CNV, energy consumption produced the largest advantage for WCRS (4.8% reduction) among the environmental impact categories investigated. In contrast to other impact categories for which pro- cesses such as animal management and waste treat- ment had large contributions, feed production and feed transport comprise the largest part of energy consumption in the dairy farming systems, and therefore the smaller energy consumption in feed transport under the WCRS system led to lower energy consumption throughout the whole life cycle of dairy farming. The present LIME results suggest that WCRS has a smaller overall environmental impact, although the total difference between the two dairy farming systems is small. In the LIME evaluation, global warming was the category in which the largest effect on the total impact was seen, followed by acidifica- tion. Therefore WCRS, which emitted more green- house gases, showed only slight overall superiority over CNV. This is not unexpected, because global warming is very likely the most emphasized environ- mental impact category in modern Japanese society, and its weighting in LIME reflects the awareness of Japanese citizens of this issue (Itsubo et al. 2004). Other advantages of rice silage are that existing paddy fields for grain rice production are available without any modification, and that rice silage pro- duction may be changed to grain rice production in case of a possible future lack of grain rice. However, these advantages are difficult to evaluate from an environmental point of view. In addition, a larger amount of the produced compost was self-consumed in WCRS. However the estimated proportions were about 36%, smaller than that of beef production, which has been reported to be 48-61% (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007b). It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. This is because of the quadratic relationship between CH4 emissions and DMI. The processes of waste treatment accounted for about 21% of the total contribution to global warming. These proportions were larger than those in Japanese beef fattening (10%) (Ogino et al. 2004) and in beef cow-calf production (11%) (Ogino et al. 2007b). This difference might be caused by the high moisture content of dairy cattle manure, which is likely to lead to large N2O and CH4 emissions. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. Many research results on the LCA of milk have been reported, and environmental impacts per kg-milk range from 689 to 1300 g CO2 equivalents for global warming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Casey & Holden 2005; Masuda et al. 2005), from 3.5 to 22.0 SO2 equivalents for acidification (Cederberg & Matts- son 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda al. 2005), from 2.8 to 28.8 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda et al. 2005), and from 1.2 to 3.9 MJ for energy consumption (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003). However, different assumptions, conditions, and emis- sion factors were applied in these different studies, and the functional units were also different (milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), and FCM). Precise comparison is therefore difficult, but the present results are almost consistent with the previously reported values. Our results suggest that use of rice silage in dairy farming has a lower overall environmental impact than the conventional system, although the difference is small. With respect to global warming, WCRS showed smaller CO2 emissions than CNV in feed trans- port due to the shorter transport distance of feed caused by the change from imported hay to regionally produced rice silage. However, a larger amount of CH4 was emitted from paddy fields of forage rice for WCRS, while CNV feed production produced no CH4 emis- sions. Therefore, WCRS was environmentally inferior for global warming due to the much larger greenhouse effect of CH4. Many research results have been

reported on methods to achieve possible reductions in CH4 emissions from rice paddy fields by water man- agement (Yagi et al. 1996; Minamikawa & Sakai 2006), management of organic matter (Yagi et al. 1997; Liou et al. 2003), and the application of specific fertilizers or materials (Bronson & Mosier 1991; Minamikawa et al. 2005). The adoption of these techniques may reduce the impact on global warming of rice production. In addition, under the WCRS system, compost made from cattle manure rather than rice straw is applied to rice paddy fields as organic fertilizer due to the use of rice as whole-crop silage, and compost application has a smaller CH4 emission than rice straw application (Yagi et al. 1997). Forage rice production therefore appears to have a smaller contribution to global warming than grain rice production, in which rice straw residues are often applied.
In contrast to the case of global warming, WCRS showed a smaller contribution to acidification and

eutrophication, and NH3 emissions from feed produc- tion were smaller for WCRS than for CNV in these impact categories. This is mainly due to the fact that rice silage production uses smaller amounts of chemi- cal fertilizer per yield than hay production, because NH3 is emitted from chemical fertilizers applied to the field in feed production. WCRS also consumed smaller amounts of energy than CNV throughout the entire dairy farming system, primarily due to the lower energy consumption for the shorter-distance feed transport in WCRS. In the comparison between WCRS and CNV, energy consumption produced the largest advantage for WCRS (4.8% reduction) among the environmental impact categories investigated. In contrast to other impact categories for which pro- cesses such as animal management and waste treat- ment had large contributions, feed production and feed transport comprise the largest part of energy consumption in the dairy farming systems, and therefore the smaller energy consumption in feed transport under the WCRS system led to lower energy consumption throughout the whole life cycle of dairy farming. The present LIME results suggest that WCRS has a smaller overall environmental impact, although the total difference between the two dairy farming systems is small. In the LIME evaluation, global warming was the category in which the largest effect on the total impact was seen, followed by acidifica- tion. Therefore WCRS, which emitted more green- house gases, showed only slight overall superiority over CNV. This is not unexpected, because global warming is very likely the most emphasized environ- mental impact category in modern Japanese society, and its weighting in LIME reflects the awareness of Japanese citizens of this issue (Itsubo et al. 2004). Other advantages of rice silage are that existing paddy fields for grain rice production are available without any modification, and that rice silage pro- duction may be changed to grain rice production in case of a possible future lack of grain rice. However, these advantages are difficult to evaluate from an environmental point of view. In addition, a larger amount of the produced compost was self-consumed in WCRS. In the dairy farming systems analyzed in this study, WCRS used 27.6% of the produced compost in contrast to CNV, in which 10.3% of the produced compost was used, and thus use of rice silage would lead to improvement of material|Environmental impacts of the dairy farming systems With respect to global warming, enteric CH4 emissions were the largest contributor in this study, as in Japanese beef production. However the estimated proportions were about 36%, smaller than that of beef production, which has been reported to be 48-61% (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007b). It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. This is because of the quadratic relationship between CH4 emissions and DMI. The processes of waste treatment accounted for about 21% of the total contribution to global warming. These proportions were larger than those in Japanese beef fattening (10%) (Ogino et al. 2004) and in beef cow-calf production (11%) (Ogino et al. 2007b). This difference might be caused by the high moisture content of dairy cattle manure, which is likely to lead to large N2O and CH4 emissions. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. Many research results on the LCA of milk have been reported, and environmental impacts per kg-milk range from 689 to 1300 g CO2 equivalents for global warming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Casey & Holden 2005; Masuda et al. 2005), from 3.5 to 22.0 SO2 equivalents for acidification (Cederberg & Matts- son 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda al. 2005), from 2.8 to 28.8 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda et al. 2005), and from 1.2 to 3.9 MJ for energy consumption (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003). However, different assumptions, conditions, and emis- sion factors were applied in these different studies, and the functional units were also different (milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), and FCM). Precise comparison is therefore difficult, but the present results are almost consistent with the previously reported values. Our results suggest that use of rice silage in dairy farming has a lower overall environmental impact than the conventional system, although the difference is small. It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. This is because of the quadratic relationship between CH4 emissions and DMI. The processes of waste treatment accounted for about 21% of the total contribution to global warming. These proportions were larger than those in Japanese beef fattening (10%) (Ogino et al. 2004) and in beef cow-calf production (11%) (Ogino et al. 2007b). This difference might be caused by the high moisture content of dairy cattle manure, which is likely to lead to large N2O and CH4 emissions. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. Many research results on the LCA of milk have been reported, and environmental impacts per kg-milk range from 689 to 1300 g CO2 equivalents for global warming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Casey & Holden 2005; Masuda et al. 2005), from 3.5 to 22.0 SO2 equivalents for acidification (Cederberg & Matts- son 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda al. 2005), from 2.8 to 28.8 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda et al. 2005), and from 1.2 to 3.9 MJ for energy consumption (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003). However, different assumptions, conditions, and emis- sion factors were applied in these different studies, and the functional units were also different (milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), and FCM). Precise comparison is therefore difficult, but the present results are almost consistent with the previously reported values. Our results suggest that use of rice silage in dairy farming has a lower overall environmental impact than the conventional system, although the difference is small. With respect to global warming, WCRS showed smaller CO2 emissions than CNV in feed trans- port due to the shorter transport distance of feed caused by the change from imported hay to regionally produced rice silage. However, a larger amount of CH4 was emitted from paddy fields of forage rice for WCRS, while CNV feed production produced no CH4 emis- sions. Therefore, WCRS was environmentally inferior for global warming due to the much larger greenhouse effect of CH4. Many research results have been

reported on methods to achieve possible reductions in CH4 emissions from rice paddy fields by water man- agement (Yagi et al. 1996; Minamikawa & Sakai 2006), management of organic matter (Yagi et al. 1997; Liou et al. 2003), and the application of specific fertilizers or materials (Bronson & Mosier 1991; Minamikawa et al. 2005). The adoption of these techniques may reduce the impact on global warming of rice production. In addition, under the WCRS system, compost made from cattle manure rather than rice straw is applied to rice paddy fields as organic fertilizer due to the use of rice as whole-crop silage, and compost application has a smaller CH4 emission than rice straw application (Yagi et al. 1997). Forage rice production therefore appears to have a smaller contribution to global warming than grain rice production, in which rice straw residues are often applied.
In contrast to the case of global warming, WCRS showed a smaller contribution to acidification and

eutrophication, and NH3 emissions from feed produc- tion were smaller for WCRS than for CNV in these impact categories. This is mainly due to the fact that rice silage production uses smaller amounts of chemi- cal fertilizer per yield than hay production, because NH3 is emitted from chemical fertilizers applied to the field in feed production. WCRS also consumed smaller amounts of energy than CNV throughout the entire dairy farming system, primarily due to the lower energy consumption for the shorter-distance feed transport in WCRS. In the comparison between WCRS and CNV, energy consumption produced the largest advantage for WCRS (4.8% reduction) among the environmental impact categories investigated. In contrast to other impact categories for which pro- cesses such as animal management and waste treat- ment had large contributions, feed production and feed transport comprise the largest part of energy consumption in the dairy farming systems, and therefore the smaller energy consumption in feed transport under the WCRS system led to lower energy consumption throughout the whole life cycle of dairy farming. The present LIME results suggest that WCRS has a smaller overall environmental impact, although the total difference between the two dairy farming systems is small. In the LIME evaluation, global warming was the category in which the largest effect on the total impact was seen, followed by acidifica- tion. Therefore WCRS, which emitted more green- house gases, showed only slight overall superiority over CNV. This is not unexpected, because global warming is very likely the most emphasized environ- mental impact category in modern Japanese society, and its weighting in LIME reflects the awareness of Japanese citizens of this issue (Itsubo et al. 2004). Other advantages of rice silage are that existing paddy fields for grain rice production are available without any modification, and that rice silage pro- duction may be changed to grain rice production in case of a possible future lack of grain rice. However, these advantages are difficult to evaluate from an environmental point of view. In addition, a larger amount of the produced compost was self-consumed in WCRS. In the dairy farming systems analyzed in this study, WCRS used 27.6% of the produced compost in contrast to CNV, in which 10.3% of the produced compost was used, and thus use of rice silage would lead to improvement of material|Environmental impacts of the dairy farming systems With respect to global warming, enteric CH4 emissions were the largest contributor in this study, as in Japanese beef production. However the estimated proportions were about 36%, smaller than that of beef production, which has been reported to be 48-61% (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007b). It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. With respect to global warming, WCRS showed smaller CO2 emissions than CNV in feed trans- port due to the shorter transport distance of feed caused by the change from imported hay to regionally produced rice silage. However, a larger amount of CH4 was emitted from paddy fields of forage rice for WCRS, while CNV feed production produced no CH4 emis- sions. Therefore, WCRS was environmentally inferior for global warming due to the much larger greenhouse effect of CH4. Many research results have been

reported on methods to achieve possible reductions in CH4 emissions from rice paddy fields by water man- agement (Yagi et al. 1996; Minamikawa & Sakai 2006), management of organic matter (Yagi et al. 1997; Liou et al. 2003), and the application of specific fertilizers or materials (Bronson & Mosier 1991; Minamikawa et al. 2005). The adoption of these techniques may reduce the impact on global warming of rice production. In addition, under the WCRS system, compost made from cattle manure rather than rice straw is applied to rice paddy fields as organic fertilizer due to the use of rice as whole-crop silage, and compost application has a smaller CH4 emission than rice straw application (Yagi et al. 1997). Forage rice production therefore appears to have a smaller contribution to global warming than grain rice production, in which rice straw residues are often applied.
In contrast to the case of global warming, WCRS showed a smaller contribution to acidification and

eutrophication, and NH3 emissions from feed produc- tion were smaller for WCRS than for CNV in these impact categories. This is mainly due to the fact that rice silage production uses smaller amounts of chemi- cal fertilizer per yield than hay production, because NH3 is emitted from chemical fertilizers applied to the field in feed production. WCRS also consumed smaller amounts of energy than CNV throughout the entire dairy farming system, primarily due to the lower energy consumption for the shorter-distance feed transport in WCRS. In the comparison between WCRS and CNV, energy consumption produced the largest advantage for WCRS (4.8% reduction) among the environmental impact categories investigated. In contrast to other impact categories for which pro- cesses such as animal management and waste treat- ment had large contributions, feed production and feed transport comprise the largest part of energy consumption in the dairy farming systems, and therefore the smaller energy consumption in feed transport under the WCRS system led to lower energy consumption throughout the whole life cycle of dairy farming. The present LIME results suggest that WCRS has a smaller overall environmental impact, although the total difference between the two dairy farming systems is small. In the LIME evaluation, global warming was the category in which the largest effect on the total impact was seen, followed by acidifica- tion. Therefore WCRS, which emitted more green- house gases, showed only slight overall superiority over CNV. This is not unexpected, because global warming is very likely the most emphasized environ- mental impact category in modern Japanese society, and its weighting in LIME reflects the awareness of Japanese citizens of this issue (Itsubo et al. 2004). Other advantages of rice silage are that existing paddy fields for grain rice production are available without any modification, and that rice silage pro- duction may be changed to grain rice production in case of a possible future lack of grain rice. However, these advantages are difficult to evaluate from an environmental point of view. In addition, a larger amount of the produced compost was self-consumed in WCRS. In the dairy farming systems analyzed in this study, WCRS used 27.6% of the produced compost in contrast to CNV, in which 10.3% of the produced compost was used, and thus use of rice silage would lead to improvement of material|Environmental impacts of the dairy farming systems With respect to global warming, enteric CH4 emissions were the largest contributor in this study, as in Japanese beef production. However the estimated proportions were about 36%, smaller than that of beef production, which has been reported to be 48-61% (Ogino et al. 2004, 2007b). It appears that the DMI of lactating COWS is higher than that of beef cattle, but increases in CH4 emissions become smaller than those of environmental loads in other processes as DMI increases. This is because of the quadratic relationship between CH4 emissions and DMI. The processes of waste treatment accounted for about 21% of the total contribution to global warming. These proportions were larger than those in Japanese beef fattening (10%) (Ogino et al. 2004) and in beef cow-calf production (11%) (Ogino et al. 2007b). This difference might be caused by the high moisture content of dairy cattle manure, which is likely to lead to large N2O and CH4 emissions. With respect to energy consumption, the proportions of animal management and waste treatment were higher in dairy farming than in Japanese beef production (Ogino et al. 2004), but they were still small. Many research results on the LCA of milk have been reported, and environmental impacts per kg-milk range from 689 to 1300 g CO2 equivalents for global warming (Cederberg & Mattsson 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Casey & Holden 2005; Masuda et al. 2005), from 3.5 to 22.0 SO2 equivalents for acidification (Cederberg & Matts- son 2000; Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda al. 2005), from 2.8 to 28.8 PO4 equivalents for eutrophication (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003; Masuda et al. 2005), and from 1.2 to 3.9 MJ for energy consumption (Haas et al. 2001; de Boer 2003; Hospido et al. 2003). However, different assumptions, conditions, and emis- sion factors were applied in these different studies, and the functional units were also different (milk, energy- corrected milk (ECM), and FCM). Precise comparison is therefore difficult, but the present results are almost consistent with the previously reported values. Our results suggest that use of rice silage in dairy farming has a lower overall environmental impact than the conventional system, although the difference is small. recycling in regions with a high density of animals relative to cultivated area.
